video.title: The Case for Multi-Strategy Hedge Fund Allocations in RIA Portfolios
video.description: Equi CIO Itay and CEO Tory discuss the case for multi-strategy hedge fund allocations in RIA portfolios, covering the correlation breakdown in 60/40 portfolios, the access gap for independent advisors, Equi's tender offer fund structure, and the institutional approach to manager selection and risk management.

key_quotes: "Look, the macro environment is as interesting as it's been in a decade." — Itay
"The key thing for advisors to understand is that the traditional 60/40 portfolio assumption — that stocks and bonds are negatively correlated — has fundamentally broken down." — Itay
"In 2022, the correlation between the S&P 500 and the Bloomberg Aggregate Bond Index hit 95%." — Itay
"That means your "diversified" portfolio was essentially a single bet." — Itay
"And this wasn't a one-month anomaly — the rolling 12-month correlation stayed above 60% for most of 2023." — Itay
"It was a feature of a specific rate environment — ZIRP, basically — and that environment is over." — Itay
"So what does that mean practically for an RIA managing, say, $3 to $5 billion for their clients?" — Tory
"If this is so obvious, why are most RIAs allocating less than 5% to alternatives?" — Tory
"I talk to advisors every week who have sophisticated, high-net-worth clients and they're running basically the same portfolio they ran in 2015." — Tory
"It's three things, and none of them are about the investment case." — Itay

clips.title: The Correlation Problem in Traditional Portfolios
clips.description: Thanks, Tory. Look, the macro environment is as interesting as it's been in a decade. We've got a Fed that's navigating a narrow path between inflation and growth, equity valuations that are stretched...
clips.key_quote: We've got a Fed that's navigating a narrow path between inflation and growth, equity valuations that are stretched by historical standards — the S&P is trading at roughly 21 times forward earnings — and a fixed income market that's still trying to figure out the new rate regime.

clips.title: The Correlation Problem in Traditional Portfolios
clips.description: In 2022, the correlation between the S&P 500 and the Bloomberg Aggregate Bond Index hit 95%. Ninety-five percent. That means your "diversified" portfolio was essentially a single bet. And this wasn't ...
clips.key_quote: And this wasn't a one-month anomaly — the rolling 12-month correlation stayed above 60% for most of 2023.

clips.title: So what does that mean practically for an RIA managing,...
clips.description: So what does that mean practically for an RIA managing, say, $3 to $5 billion for their clients?
clips.key_quote: So what does that mean practically for an RIA managing, say, $3 to $5 billion for their clients?

clips.title: Let's talk about the elephant in the room though.
clips.description: Let's talk about the elephant in the room though. If this is so obvious, why are most RIAs allocating less than 5% to alternatives? I talk to advisors every week who have sophisticated, high-net-worth...
clips.key_quote: I talk to advisors every week who have sophisticated, high-net-worth clients and they're running basically the same portfolio they ran in 2015.

clips.title: Why RIAs Are Underallocated to Alternatives
clips.description: It's three things, and none of them are about the investment case. First, access — the best multi-strategy managers have $5 to $10 million minimums, sometimes higher. An RIA managing $3 billion across...
clips.key_quote: And third, operations — managing LP agreements, capital calls, K-1s, side pockets, redemption queues across multiple hedge fund positions is an operational nightmare for a firm that's built to run model portfolios.
